1	---
2	ddx:
3	  id: PLAN-2026-05-06-prose-quality-integration
4	  depends_on:
5	    - FEAT-027
6	    - TD-036
7	    - TD-037
8	    - ADR-025
9	  status: draft
10	---
11	# Plan: Prose Quality Integration with Vale
12	
13	## Goal
14	
15	DDx should improve the document side of the project automatically. Prose checks
16	should make docs more actionable, clearer, and cheaper to feed to agents by
17	removing empty words where doing so preserves meaning. The public surface stays
18	`ddx doc prose`; Vale is the pinned deterministic engine behind that surface
19	per ADR-025.
20	
21	The checker is not an AI detector and not a generic style linter. It exists to
22	catch prose that makes DDx documents harder to execute, review, trust, or carry
23	through an agent context window.
24	
25	## Product Contract
26	
27	- `ddx doc prose` is the only public prose checker surface.
28	- Vale-specific rule names, config paths, and install details stay behind DDx
29	  output and diagnostics.
30	- Docs under `docs/**` are checked by default.
31	- Findings are advisory by default.
32	- Findings preserve DDx terms, commands, paths, headings, tables, frontmatter,
33	  IDs, acceptance criteria, and legitimate technical density.
34	- Suggestions prefer shorter wording when the shorter wording is equally
35	  specific.
36	
37	## Implementation Phases
38	
39	### Phase 1: Pinned Vale Health
40	
41	Implement DDx's Vale installation contract.
42	
43	Work:
44	
45	- Add a prose-checker health check to `ddx doctor`.
46	- Check `exec.LookPath("vale")`.
47	- Run `vale --version`.
48	- Accept only `vale version 3.13.0` for the initial implementation.
49	- In verbose mode, print the resolved Vale path and version.
50	- When missing or unsupported, report a prose-checker diagnostic that names the
51	  pinned version and points to Vale's official install/release channel.
52	
53	Acceptance:
54	
55	- A test covers missing `vale`.
56	- A test covers wrong-version `vale`.
57	- A test covers supported-version `vale`.
58	- `ddx doctor` remains non-fatal until the Vale-backed prose command lands.
59	
60	### Phase 2: DDx Vale Rule Pack
61	
62	Replace the current embedded-rule YAML shape with Vale styles owned by DDx.
63	
64	Work:
65	
66	- Add `library/checks/prose-quality/styles/DDx/*.yml`.
67	- Keep `library/checks/prose-quality/check.yaml` as DDx metadata: mode,
68	  policy, includes, excludes, pinned Vale version, and rule-pack version.
69	- Define initial DDx Vale rules:
70	  - unsupported broad claim
71	  - LLM-default polish / AI slop
72	  - filler transition
73	  - missing actor/action/artifact cue
74	  - repeated generic opening
75	  - token-cost reduction candidate
76	- Keep project vocabulary in DDx metadata and render it into Vale vocab/config
77	  when invoking Vale.
78	
79	Acceptance:
80	
81	- Rule pack validates with Vale 3.13.0.
82	- Rule files are shipped by `ddx init` / default plugin update.
83	- Existing DDx terms remain accepted by default.
84	
85	Rule-pack contents:
86	
87	| Vale file | DDx rule id | Purpose | Example finding |
88	|---|---|---|---|
89	| `DDx/UnsupportedClaim.yml` | `prose.claim.unsupported` | Broad praise or capability claim without concrete subject, mechanism, or evidence. | "This robust system creates a seamless workflow." |
90	| `DDx/AISlop.yml` | `prose.ai_slop.polish` | LLM-default polish words and phrases when they replace useful detail. | "This unlocks powerful, sophisticated automation." |
91	| `DDx/FillerTransition.yml` | `prose.filler.transition` | Throat-clearing transitions that can be removed without losing meaning. | "It is important to note that..." |
92	| `DDx/MissingActorAction.yml` | `prose.specificity.actor_action` | Sentences that say a process "enables", "supports", or "streamlines" without naming the actor, action, artifact, or boundary. | "This enables better collaboration across the workflow." |
93	| `DDx/TokenCost.yml` | `prose.cost.filler` | Removable intensifiers, duplicate summaries, or empty framing that increase token cost without adding execution value. | "In order to effectively begin to..." |
94	| `DDx/RepeatedOpening.yml` | `prose.structure.repeated_opening` | Repeated opening sentence shapes or duplicated lead-ins that read like generated filler. | "This document provides..." repeated across adjacent paragraphs. |
95	| `DDx/Vocabulary.yml` | `prose.vocabulary.generic_substitute` | Project-local generic substitutes that hide DDx terms. | "task" where the document means "bead", if configured. |
96	
97	Rule implementation notes:
98	
99	- Use Vale existence/substitution rules for deterministic phrase detection.
100	- Use Vale scopes and token ignores to skip code spans, fenced blocks, links,
101	  and frontmatter.
102	- Use DDx normalization to merge multiple word-level Vale hits on the same line
103	  into one DDx finding when the sentence has one underlying problem.
104	- Keep rule messages short; DDx owns the longer rationale and suggested edit in
105	  the normalization layer.
106	- Avoid default "bad word" behavior. A word becomes a finding only when the rule
107	  description says why it weakens DDx execution, review, trust, or token cost.
108	
109	### Phase 3: Vale Adapter and Normalization
110	
111	Make `ddx doc prose` invoke Vale while returning DDx findings.
112	
113	Work:
114	
115	- Generate a temporary `.vale.ini` from DDx defaults and project config.
116	- Point `StylesPath` at the packaged DDx Vale styles.
117	- Pass explicit paths for `--changed` and direct-path mode.
118	- Invoke `vale --config <tmp> --output=JSON --no-global`.
119	- Parse Vale JSON into `docprose.Finding`.
120	- Map Vale checks to DDx rule IDs and DDx wording.
121	- Preserve the embedded checker only as a test fallback or fixture generator,
122	  not as the public default.
123	
124	Acceptance:
125	
126	- `ddx doc prose --changed` works with no project `.vale.ini`.
127	- `ddx doc prose <paths>` works with no project `.vale.ini`.
128	- Missing/unsupported Vale produces a DDx prose-checker diagnostic.
129	- User-facing output contains DDx rule IDs and DDx suggestions, not raw Vale
130	  implementation details.
131	
132	### Phase 4: Evaluation Corpus
133	
134	Use the combined tool insight to tune Vale rules against real DDx docs.
135	
136	Work:
137	
138	- Create a labeled corpus under `cli/internal/docprose/testdata/corpus/`.
139	- Include real DDx excerpts and synthetic edge cases.
140	- Label each expected issue:
141	  - useful finding
142	  - false positive
143	  - intentionally dense technical prose
144	  - token-cost reduction candidate
145	  - missed AI-slop construction
146	- Include negative samples for paths, commands, tables, IDs, frontmatter, API
147	  names, acceptance criteria, and measured claims.
148	- Record expected findings in golden JSON.
149	
150	Acceptance:
151	
152	- Corpus tests compare normalized DDx findings, not Vale raw output.
153	- Good technical samples stay quiet.
154	- AI-slop samples produce line-specific findings.
155	- Token-cost samples identify removable filler without changing meaning.
156	- Full `docs/**` scan reports a plausible number of findings and no broad
157	  technical-structure noise.
158	
159	Corpus contents:
160	
161	| Corpus group | Purpose | Minimum cases |
162	|---|---|---:|
163	| `positive/ai-slop` | Common LLM constructions that sound polished but omit useful detail. | 12 |
164	| `positive/unsupported-claim` | Broad claims that need actor/action/evidence. | 10 |
165	| `positive/token-cost` | Sentences where shorter wording is strictly better. | 10 |
166	| `positive/missing-actor-action` | Abstract "enables/supports/streamlines" sentences. | 8 |
167	| `negative/technical-density` | Dense but useful DDx technical prose. | 10 |
168	| `negative/markdown-structure` | Tables, commands, paths, links, frontmatter, IDs, code spans. | 12 |
169	| `negative/evidence-backed-claim` | Strong claims backed by tests, benchmarks, measurements, or explicit constraints. | 8 |
170	| `real/ddx-docs` | Excerpts from existing DDx docs with reviewed labels. | 30 |
171	
172	Evaluation metrics:
173	
174	| Metric | Gate |
175	|---|---|
176	| Positive recall | At least 80% of labeled positive cases produce the expected DDx rule id. |
177	| Negative quiet rate | At least 90% of negative cases produce no finding. |
178	| Structure false positives | 0 findings in code spans, fenced blocks, paths, commands, frontmatter, and DDx IDs unless the case is explicitly positive. |
179	| Token-cost precision | At least 80% of token-cost findings are judged shorter-without-meaning-loss. |
180	| Real-doc usefulness | A manual review of the full-doc scan marks at least 70% of findings useful. |
181	| Finding volume | Full `docs/**` scan is sparse enough for review: target under 1 finding per 1,000 words until rules prove higher precision. |
182	
183	How we know it works:
184	
185	- Golden tests lock expected normalized findings for the corpus.
186	- A full `docs/**` report is reviewed and labeled before enabling workflow
187	  hooks.
188	- Each rule has both positive and negative examples; no rule ships with only
189	  synthetic positives.
190	- Token-count improvements are reported separately from clarity findings so
191	  terse-but-ambiguous rewrites do not pass as quality improvements.
192	
193	### Phase 5: Agent Skill Upgrade
194	
195	Make agents use the prose workflow without reminders.
196	
197	Work:
198	
199	- Update `human-writing-support` to treat Vale-backed `ddx doc prose` as the
200	  normal post-edit check.
201	- Add examples for:
202	  - unsupported benefit claim -> concrete rewrite
203	  - AI-slop paragraph -> shorter actionable sentence
204	  - false positive in a table/path/API sample -> leave unchanged
205	  - token-cost edit -> remove filler while preserving meaning
206	- Instruct agents to rerun `ddx doc prose --changed` after edits.
207	- Instruct agents to report intentional exceptions only when findings remain.
208	
209	Acceptance:
210	
211	- Skill validation passes.
212	- A docs-edit dry run shows the agent runs the prose check, applies obvious
213	  edits, and leaves legitimate technical prose intact.
214	
215	Skill contents:
216	
217	- Trigger: any writing, rewriting, editing, or reviewing of Markdown under
218	  `docs/`.
219	- Required workflow:
220	  1. preserve the author's intent and DDx terminology;
221	  2. edit the document;
222	  3. run `ddx doc prose --changed`;
223	  4. apply high-signal findings;
224	  5. rerun `ddx doc prose --changed`;
225	  6. summarize remaining intentional exceptions.
226	- Preservation rules:
227	  - keep paths, commands, IDs, frontmatter, headings, table structure, API
228	    names, and acceptance criteria intact;
229	  - do not flatten useful technical density;
230	  - do not rewrite quoted source text unless the task is explicitly about that
231	    quote.
232	- Rewrite guidance:
233	  - replace broad claims with actor/action/artifact/evidence;
234	  - delete filler transitions when the sentence still reads correctly;
235	  - prefer shorter wording when it is equally precise;
236	  - split overstuffed sentences only when doing so improves reviewability;
237	  - leave a finding unresolved when the flagged text is precise and explain why.
238	- Examples:
239	  - unsupported benefit claim before/after;
240	  - AI-slop paragraph before/after;
241	  - token-cost reduction before/after with word-count delta;
242	  - false positive that should be ignored;
243	  - table/path/API sample that must not be rewritten.
244	
245	Skill evals:
246	
247	| Eval | Prompt | Pass condition |
248	|---|---|---|
249	| `docs-edit-runs-check` | Edit a small doc under `docs/`. | Agent runs `ddx doc prose --changed` after editing. |
250	| `fixes-ai-slop` | Given a doc with broad LLM-default claims. | Agent rewrites to concrete actor/action/evidence and reruns the check. |
251	| `preserves-technical-structure` | Given a doc with paths, commands, tables, IDs, and AC. | Agent leaves structure intact and does not chase false positives. |
252	| `reduces-token-cost` | Given filler-heavy prose with clear meaning. | Agent shortens prose without losing constraints or evidence. |
253	| `reports-exceptions` | Given a legitimate finding that should remain. | Agent states the exception and rationale instead of silently ignoring it. |
254	
255	How we know the skill works:
256	
257	- The eval prompts run through the same skill-loading path agents use in normal
258	  work.
259	- Each eval checks behavior, not exact prose: command was run, findings were
260	  handled, structure was preserved, and remaining exceptions were explained.
261	- A failed eval produces a concrete skill patch, not a checker-rule patch,
262	  unless the underlying deterministic finding is wrong.
263	
264	### Phase 6: Workflow Hooks
265	
266	Run prose checks in normal DDx work without making users ask.
267	
268	Work:
269	
270	- When a `ddx try` / `ddx work` attempt changes Markdown under `docs/`, run
271	  `ddx doc prose --changed` before finalization.
272	- Include prose findings in execution evidence.
273	- Feed findings back to the agent before final response when the attempt is
274	  still editable.
275	- Keep correctness review and prose findings separate.
276	- Keep pre-commit advisory-only unless a later ADR changes policy.
277	
278	Acceptance:
279	
280	- Docs-changing attempts attach prose-check evidence.
281	- Advisory prose findings do not block bead closure by default.
282	- Agents get findings early enough to fix obvious prose issues.
283	- Operator-facing output stays concise.
284	
285	## Rule Strategy
286	
287	DDx should use Vale for deterministic matching and Markdown awareness, but DDx
288	owns the quality model. Rules should target document usefulness, not generic
289	style preferences.
290	
291	Initial rule families:
292	
293	| Rule family | Flags | Does not flag |
294	|---|---|---|
295	| Unsupported broad claim | "robust", "comprehensive", "seamless", "powerful" when no concrete actor/action/evidence follows | measured claims, concrete test matrices, named capabilities |
296	| AI-slop construction | fluent polish that omits actor, action, artifact, boundary, or evidence | precise summary of concrete behavior |
297	| Filler transition | "it is important to note", "in today's landscape", "to be clear" when removable | quoted source text, intentional public copy if still specific |
298	| Missing actor/action/artifact | sentence says a thing "enables" or "supports" without naming what changes | API descriptions with explicit subject and behavior |
299	| Token-cost reduction | removable intensifiers, throat-clearing, duplicate summary sentences | necessary definitions, acceptance criteria, legal/security constraints |
300	
301	## Token-Cost Policy
302	
303	Lower word count is beneficial when it preserves or improves specificity. The
304	checker should prefer edits that remove filler and duplicate phrasing, but it
305	must not compress away:
306	
307	- acceptance criteria;
308	- command examples;
309	- file paths and IDs;
310	- rationale needed for future review;
311	- constraints that prevent agent drift;
312	- evidence that supports a decision.
313	
314	The evaluation corpus should track token-cost candidates separately from
315	clarity findings so DDx can tune this without encouraging terse but ambiguous
316	docs.
317	
318	## Bead Breakdown
319	
320	This plan is not one bead. It is an epic with dependency-ordered child beads.
321	Each child should be executable on its own, with a narrow file scope and a
322	small verification surface.
323	
324	Some child beads require higher-judgment execution: corpus labeling, rule
325	semantics, skill behavior, and workflow-hook policy. Those beads should use the
326	bead-level execution-hint policy in TD-037 rather than ad hoc model or harness
327	choices. In particular, use `triage.estimated_difficulty=hard` for beads that
328	need stronger reasoning, and keep durable harness/provider/model pins out of
329	scope for this plan.
330	
331	### Epic
332	
333	`prose: integrate Vale-backed DDx prose quality`
334	
335	Acceptance:
336	
337	1. `ddx doctor` reports pinned Vale health.
338	2. `ddx doc prose --changed` uses Vale-backed DDx rules with no project
339	   `.vale.ini`.
340	3. Normalized findings use DDx rule IDs, rationales, and suggested edits.
341	4. Labeled corpus tests prove useful findings and low structural noise.
342	5. `human-writing-support` tells agents how to run and apply the workflow.
343	6. Docs-changing DDx attempts attach advisory prose-check evidence.
344	
345	### Child Beads
346	
347	| Order | Bead | Scope | Suggested hint | Depends on |
348	|---:|---|---|---|---|
349	| 0 | `try: define and enforce bead-level execution hints` | Implement TD-037 parsing, lint, evidence, and metrics slices needed before hard difficulty hints are relied on. | `triage.estimated_difficulty=hard` | none |
350	| 1 | `doctor: validate pinned Vale prose checker` | `cli/cmd/doctor.go`, doctor tests, constants for Vale version. | none | 0 |
351	| 2 | `docprose: add DDx Vale style pack skeleton` | `library/checks/prose-quality/styles/DDx/`, metadata schema, no command wiring. | none | 1 |
352	| 3 | `docprose: add corpus harness for normalized findings` | `cli/internal/docprose/testdata/corpus/`, corpus loader/tests, expected JSON schema. | `triage.estimated_difficulty=hard` for corpus design judgment | 2 |
353	| 4 | `docprose: port initial rules to Vale styles` | Vale style files and corpus golden cases for unsupported claim, AI slop, filler, token cost. | `triage.estimated_difficulty=hard` for rule precision judgment | 3 |
354	| 5 | `docprose: generate temporary Vale config` | config generation from DDx defaults/project config; no Vale execution yet. | none | 2 |
355	| 6 | `docprose: invoke Vale and parse JSON` | Vale subprocess adapter, JSON structs, error diagnostics. | none | 5 |
356	| 7 | `docprose: normalize Vale findings to DDx findings` | rule-id mapping, rationale/suggested-edit mapping, line merge behavior. | `triage.estimated_difficulty=hard` for finding-quality semantics | 4, 6 |
357	| 8 | `doc prose: switch command to Vale-backed engine` | `ddx doc prose --changed` and explicit path behavior. | none | 7 |
358	| 9 | `bead review: reuse Vale-backed prose findings` | `ddx bead review --prose` path, review evidence. | `triage.estimated_difficulty=hard` for review-policy interaction | 8 |
359	| 10 | `skills: upgrade human-writing-support workflow examples` | active and shipped skill copies, eval prompts if available. | `triage.estimated_difficulty=hard` for skill behavior quality | 8 |
360	| 11 | `try: attach prose-check evidence for docs-changing attempts` | execute/try/work evidence capture and advisory handling. | `triage.estimated_difficulty=hard` for workflow-hook policy | 8, 10 |
361	| 12 | `docs: run Vale-backed prose pass across DDx docs` | Apply high-signal findings to `docs/**`; record before/after finding count and word-count delta. | `triage.estimated_difficulty=hard` for corpus-scale editorial judgment | 11 |
362	
363	### Split Rules
364	
365	- If a bead would touch both command plumbing and rule semantics, split it.
366	- If a bead would require changing execution flow and docs content, split it.
367	- If corpus labels change because rule behavior changes, keep that with the
368	  rule bead that caused the change.
369	- Do not file the full-doc cleanup bead until the checker, skill, and workflow
370	  evidence path are working.
371	
372	Each bead should reference ADR-025 and this plan via `spec-id` or inline
373	context. The first implementation bead should start with `ddx doctor` because
374	the engine decision depends on a trustworthy Vale binary.

15	func (f *CommandFactory) newBeadReviewCommand() *cobra.Command {
16		cmd := &cobra.Command{
17			Use:   "review <id>",
18			Short: "Generate a review prompt for a bead's implementation",
19			Long: "Generate a review-ready prompt for a bead implementation.\n\n" +
20				"The prompt includes:\n" +
21				"  - Bead title, description, and acceptance criteria\n" +
22				"  - Full content of governing documents (spec-id refs from the bead)\n" +
23				"  - Git diff of the reviewed commit (git show)\n" +
24				"  - Review instructions with the expected APPROVE/REQUEST_CHANGES/BLOCK output contract\n\n" +
25				"Use --prose to add an advisory prose-quality section derived from the bead\n" +
26				"text, using the same deterministic prose checker as `ddx doc prose`.\n\n" +
27				"By default the commit is taken from the bead's closing_commit_sha field.\n" +
28				"Use --from-rev to override.\n\n" +
29				"Pipe the output into `ddx run`:\n\n" +
30				"  ddx bead review <id> | ddx run --prompt @-\n" +
31				"  ddx bead review <id> --output /tmp/review.md && ddx run --prompt /tmp/review.md",
32			Args: cobra.ExactArgs(1),
33			RunE: f.runBeadReview,
34		}
35		cmd.Flags().String("from-rev", "", "Commit SHA to review (default: closing_commit_sha from bead)")
36		cmd.Flags().Int("iter", 1, "Review iteration number (shown in prompt header and grade table)")
37		cmd.Flags().String("output", "", "Write prompt to file instead of stdout")
38		cmd.Flags().Bool("prose", false, "Include advisory prose findings derived from the bead text")
39		return cmd
40	}
41	
42	func (f *CommandFactory) runBeadReview(cmd *cobra.Command, args []string) error {
43		beadID := args[0]
44		fromRev, _ := cmd.Flags().GetString("from-rev")
45		iter, _ := cmd.Flags().GetInt("iter")
46		outputFile, _ := cmd.Flags().GetString("output")
47		includeProse, _ := cmd.Flags().GetBool("prose")
48	
49		s := f.beadStore()
50		b, err := s.Get(context.Background(), beadID)
51		if err != nil {
52			return err
53		}
54	
55		// Resolve the commit SHA to review.
56		// Priority: --from-rev flag > closing_commit_sha on the bead.
57		rev := strings.TrimSpace(fromRev)
58		if rev == "" {
59			if sha, ok := b.Extra["closing_commit_sha"].(string); ok {
60				rev = strings.TrimSpace(sha)
61			}
62		}
63		if rev == "" {
64			return fmt.Errorf("no commit to review: use --from-rev <sha>, or close the bead with a commit SHA first (ddx bead close --commit <sha>)")
65		}
66	
67		// Project root is used for git operations and governing doc file reads.
68		projectRoot := gitpkg.FindProjectRoot(f.WorkingDir)
69		if projectRoot == "" {
70			projectRoot = f.WorkingDir
71		}
72	
73		// Fetch the git diff for the commit.
74		diff, err := beadReviewGitShow(projectRoot, rev)
75		if err != nil {
76			return fmt.Errorf("git show %s: %w", rev, err)
77		}
78	
79		// Resolve governing document references from the bead's spec-id field.
80		refs := agent.ResolveGoverningRefs(projectRoot, b)
81	
82		// Delegate prompt assembly to the single source of truth in the agent
83		// package so the CLI handler and the post-merge reviewer cannot drift.
84		var prompt string
85		if includeProse {
86			prompt = agent.BuildReviewPromptWithProse(b, iter, rev, diff, projectRoot, refs)
87		} else {
88			prompt = agent.BuildReviewPrompt(b, iter, rev, diff, projectRoot, refs)
89		}
90	
91		if outputFile != "" {
92			return os.WriteFile(outputFile, []byte(prompt), 0o644)
93		}
94		_, err = fmt.Fprint(cmd.OutOrStdout(), prompt)
95		return err
96	}
97	
98	// beadReviewGitShow runs `git show <rev>` with pathspec exclusions for
99	// execution-evidence noise so the review prompt's <diff> section stays
100	// bounded. See agent.EvidenceReviewExcludePathspecs and ddx-39e27896.
101	func beadReviewGitShow(projectRoot, rev string) (string, error) {
102		ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), 30*time.Second)
103		defer cancel()
104		args := append([]string{"show", rev, "--", "."}, agent.EvidenceReviewExcludePathspecs()...)
105		out, err := gitpkg.Command(ctx, projectRoot, args...).Output()
106		if err != nil {
107			return "", err
108		}
109		return string(out), nil
110	}

23	// beadStrictnessMode returns the strictness mode for review based on bead
24	// labels. kind:doc and kind:mechanical → mechanical; kind:refactor and
25	// kind:chore → behavior-light; default → strict.
26	func beadStrictnessMode(labels []string) string {
27		for _, l := range labels {
28			switch l {
29			case "kind:doc", "kind:mechanical":
30				return StrictnessMechanical
31			case "kind:refactor", "kind:chore":
32				return StrictnessBehavior
33			}
34		}
35		return StrictnessStrict
36	}

44		case StrictnessBehavior:
45			return "behavior-light — build green plus file/symbol evidence suffices; test-name match required only when an AC explicitly names a Test* function."

74	// acCheckOutput is the minimal shape of .ddx/executions/<id>/ac-check.json
75	// needed for AC-grade mismatch detection.
76	type acCheckOutput struct {
77		Items []struct {
78			AC     int    `json:"ac"`
79			Result string `json:"result"`
80		} `json:"items"`
81	}
82	
83	// countACGradeMismatches compares the reviewer's per-AC grades against
84	// the mechanical results from ac-check.json. Returns the number of mismatches
85	// (reviewer says pass where ac-check says fail, or vice versa) and a list
86	// of per-AC reasons. Ignores needs_judgment and error mechanical results.
87	func countACGradeMismatches(acCheckJSONStr string, perAC []ReviewAC) (int, []string) {
88		if acCheckJSONStr == "" || len(perAC) == 0 {
89			return 0, nil
90		}
91		var check acCheckOutput
92		if err := json.Unmarshal([]byte(acCheckJSONStr), &check); err != nil {
93			return 0, nil
94		}
95		mechByNum := make(map[int]string, len(check.Items))
96		for _, item := range check.Items {
97			mechByNum[item.AC] = strings.ToLower(item.Result)
98		}
99		count := 0
100		var reasons []string
101		for _, ac := range perAC {
102			mech, ok := mechByNum[ac.Number]
103			if !ok {
104				continue
105			}
106			// Only compare definitive mechanical verdicts; skip needs_judgment/error.
107			if mech != "pass" && mech != "fail" {
108				continue
109			}
110			grade := strings.ToLower(ac.Grade)
111			if (mech == "fail" && grade == "pass") || (mech == "pass" && grade == "fail") {
112				count++
113				reasons = append(reasons, fmt.Sprintf("ac%d: ac_check=%s reviewer=%s", ac.Number, mech, grade))
114			}
115		}
116		return count, reasons
117	}

307	// beadReviewInstructions is the review contract embedded in the prompt.
308	// The reviewing agent must produce a single JSON object matching the
309	// ReviewVerdict schema (schema_version: 1). The markdown contract this
310	// replaces silently mis-parsed `### Verdict: APPROVE` outputs whenever the
311	// model echoed the prompt's options header — see review_verdict.go.
312	const beadReviewInstructions = `You are reviewing a bead implementation against its acceptance criteria.
313	
314	## AC-Check Ratification
315	
316	When an <ac-check> section is present, ratify the mechanical results rather
317	than re-verifying them independently from the diff:
318	
319	- result="pass": confirm the evidence is credible. Override to fail only if
320	  the evidence is fabricated — include judgment_override_reason and a diff
321	  citation (file:line) in the per_ac evidence string.
322	- result="fail": mechanically verified failure. Grade as fail and BLOCK unless
323	  the commit message contains an explicit AC-Waive trailer for this AC.
324	- result="needs_judgment": adjudicate from the diff. If you cannot determine
325	  pass/fail without additional bead context from the operator, use
326	  REQUEST_CLARIFICATION for that AC item.
327	- result="error": treat as needs_judgment.
328	
329	Overriding a mechanical grade (pass→fail or fail→pass) requires an explicit
330	judgment_override_reason note and a concrete diff citation in the evidence.
331	
332	## Strictness Mode
333	
334	The <strictness-mode> tag specifies per-bead evidence requirements:
335	
336	- strict (kind:fix, kind:feat): each AC must be anchored to a named Test*
337	  function or a diff-touched symbol; file-only evidence is insufficient.
338	- behavior-light (kind:refactor, kind:chore): build green plus file/symbol
339	  evidence suffices; test-name match required only when an AC explicitly
340	  names a Test* function.
341	- mechanical (kind:doc, kind:mechanical): file presence, renames, or symbol
342	  evidence only; no test-name or runtime evidence required.
343	
344	## Verdicts
345	
346	For each acceptance-criteria (AC) item, decide whether it is implemented
347	correctly, then assign one overall verdict:
348	
349	- APPROVE — every AC item is fully and correctly implemented.
350	- REQUEST_CHANGES — some AC items are partial or have fixable minor issues.
351	- BLOCK — at least one AC item is not implemented or incorrectly implemented;
352	  or the diff is insufficient to evaluate.
353	- REQUEST_CLARIFICATION — you cannot adjudicate one or more needs_judgment AC
354	  items without operator clarification. Use this ONLY when the item is
355	  ambiguous even given the full diff. This verdict does NOT block the queue;
356	  it routes to the operator lane for input.
357	
358	## Required output format (schema_version: 1)
359	
360	Respond with EXACTLY one JSON object as your final response, fenced as a single ` + "`" + `` + "`" + `` + "`" + `json … ` + "`" + `` + "`" + `` + "`" + ` code block. Do not include any prose outside the fenced block. The JSON must match this schema:
361

363	{
364	  "schema_version": 1,
365	  "verdict": "APPROVE",
366	  "summary": "≤300 char human-readable verdict justification",
367	  "per_ac": [
368	    { "number": 1, "item": "acceptance criterion text", "grade": "pass", "evidence": "file:line or test evidence" }
369	  ],

372	  ]

375

377	- "verdict" must be exactly one of "APPROVE", "REQUEST_CHANGES", "BLOCK", "REQUEST_CLARIFICATION".
378	- "severity" must be exactly one of "info", "warn", "block".
379	- Output the JSON object inside ONE fenced ` + "`" + `` + "`" + `` + "`" + `json … ` + "`" + `` + "`" + `` + "`" + ` block. No additional prose, no extra fences, no markdown headings.

382	// BuildReviewPromptOptions configures evidence-bounded prompt assembly.
383	// FEAT-022 §5/§7: caps drive per-section trimming and the pre-dispatch
384	// short-circuit on residual oversize.
385	type BuildReviewPromptOptions struct {
386		Caps evidence.Caps
387		// ACCheckJSON, when non-empty, is the raw JSON content of the
388		// .ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/ac-check.json file produced by
389		// `ddx bead ac-check`. Injected as a structured <ac-check> section so
390		// the reviewer ratifies mechanical results rather than re-grepping the diff.
391		ACCheckJSON string
392	}
393	
394	// ReviewPromptOptions controls optional prompt sections.
395	type ReviewPromptOptions struct {
396		IncludeProse bool
397	}
398	
399	// BuildReviewPromptResult is the structured output of BuildReviewPromptBounded.
400	// Overflow is true when, after all per-section trimming, the assembled
401	// prompt still exceeds Caps.MaxPromptBytes; callers MUST NOT dispatch in
402	// that case (FEAT-022 §7).
403	type BuildReviewPromptResult struct {
404		Prompt   string
405		Overflow bool
406		Sections []evidence.EvidenceAssemblySection
407	}
408	
409	// BuildReviewPrompt builds the complete review prompt for a bead implementation
410	// using default caps. Callers needing overflow detection use
411	// BuildReviewPromptBounded.
412	func BuildReviewPrompt(b *bead.Bead, iter int, rev, diff, projectRoot string, refs []GoverningRef) string {
413		return BuildReviewPromptBounded(b, iter, rev, diff, projectRoot, refs, BuildReviewPromptOptions{Caps: evidence.DefaultCaps()}).Prompt
414	}
415	
416	// BuildReviewPromptWithProse builds the review prompt and includes an
417	// advisory prose-quality section derived from the bead text using the same
418	// docprose checker and config path as `ddx doc prose`.
419	func BuildReviewPromptWithProse(b *bead.Bead, iter int, rev, diff, projectRoot string, refs []GoverningRef) string {
420		return BuildReviewPromptBoundedWithProse(b, iter, rev, diff, projectRoot, refs, BuildReviewPromptOptions{Caps: evidence.DefaultCaps()}).Prompt
421	}
422	
423	// BuildReviewPromptBounded assembles the review prompt under byte caps using
424	// the cli/internal/evidence primitives. Governing documents are read via
425	// evidence.ReadFileClamped, the diff is decomposed and clamped via
426	// evidence.ClampDiff, and per-file inlining is bounded by
427	// Caps.MaxInlinedFileBytes. The minimum evidence floor (bead title,
428	// description, acceptance, notes, and the full changed-file inventory) is
429	// preserved verbatim regardless of cap pressure (FEAT-022 §5).
430	func BuildReviewPromptBounded(b *bead.Bead, iter int, rev, diff, projectRoot string, refs []GoverningRef, opts BuildReviewPromptOptions) BuildReviewPromptResult {
431		return buildReviewPromptBounded(b, iter, rev, diff, projectRoot, refs, opts, false)
432	}
433	
434	// BuildReviewPromptBoundedWithProse assembles the review prompt with the
435	// optional prose-quality section enabled.
436	func BuildReviewPromptBoundedWithProse(b *bead.Bead, iter int, rev, diff, projectRoot string, refs []GoverningRef, opts BuildReviewPromptOptions) BuildReviewPromptResult {
437		return buildReviewPromptBounded(b, iter, rev, diff, projectRoot, refs, opts, true)
438	}
439	
440	func buildReviewPromptBounded(b *bead.Bead, iter int, rev, diff, projectRoot string, refs []GoverningRef, opts BuildReviewPromptOptions, includeProse bool) BuildReviewPromptResult {
441		caps := opts.Caps
442		if caps.MaxPromptBytes == 0 {
443			caps = evidence.DefaultCaps()
444		}
445	
446		var sb strings.Builder
447		sections := []evidence.EvidenceAssemblySection{}
448	
449		sb.WriteString("<bead-review>\n")
450	
451		// ── Bead section (floor) ────────────────────────────────────────────────
452		beadStart := sb.Len()
453		fmt.Fprintf(&sb, "  <bead id=%q iter=%d>\n", b.ID, iter)
454		fmt.Fprintf(&sb, "    <title>%s</title>\n", reviewXMLEscape(strings.TrimSpace(b.Title)))
455	
456		if desc := strings.TrimSpace(b.Description); desc != "" {
457			fmt.Fprintf(&sb, "    <description>\n%s\n    </description>\n", reviewXMLEscape(desc))
458		} else {
459			sb.WriteString("    <description/>\n")
460		}
461	
462		if acc := strings.TrimSpace(b.Acceptance); acc != "" {
463			fmt.Fprintf(&sb, "    <acceptance>\n%s\n    </acceptance>\n", reviewXMLEscape(acc))
464		} else {
465			sb.WriteString("    <acceptance/>\n")
466		}
467	
468		if notes := strings.TrimSpace(b.Notes); notes != "" {
469			fmt.Fprintf(&sb, "    <notes>\n%s\n    </notes>\n", reviewXMLEscape(notes))
470		}
471	
472		if len(b.Labels) > 0 {
473			fmt.Fprintf(&sb, "    <labels>%s</labels>\n", reviewXMLEscape(strings.Join(b.Labels, ", ")))
474		}
475	
476		sb.WriteString("  </bead>\n\n")
477		sections = append(sections, evidence.EvidenceAssemblySection{
478			Name:          "bead",
479			BytesIncluded: sb.Len() - beadStart,
480			SelectedItems: []string{"bead"},
481		})
482	
483		// ── Changed-file inventory (floor) ──────────────────────────────────────
484		allFiles := evidence.DecomposeDiff(diff)
485		if len(allFiles) > 0 {
486			invStart := sb.Len()
487			sb.WriteString("  <changed-files>\n")
488			paths := make([]string, 0, len(allFiles))
489			for _, f := range allFiles {
490				fmt.Fprintf(&sb, "    <file>%s</file>\n", reviewXMLEscape(f.Path))
491				paths = append(paths, f.Path)
492			}
493			sb.WriteString("  </changed-files>\n\n")
494			sections = append(sections, evidence.EvidenceAssemblySection{
495				Name:          "changed-files",
496				BytesIncluded: sb.Len() - invStart,
497				SelectedItems: paths,
498			})
499		}
